title_en: Teachings Handed Down by the Master
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I have heard that the former masters kept certain things in their hearts that were never set down in prescriptions. I wish to hear them, store them in mind, and pattern my conduct upon them — using them above to govern the people, and below to govern my own body — so that the common people might be free of illness, high and low might live in harmony and affection, virtue and beneficence might flow down to them, their descendants might be free of care, and this might be handed down to later generations without end. May I hear of this?' Qibo said: 'How far-reaching a question this is! Governing the people and governing oneself, governing others and governing this body, governing the small and governing the great, governing a state and governing a household — in none of these can one succeed by going against what is proper; only by following it can one succeed. And "following" does not refer solely to yin and yang and the channels, to discoursing on the compliance or rebellion of qi — the common people, too, all wish to have their own intentions followed.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one follow this?' Qibo said: 'On entering a state, ask about its customs; on entering a household, ask about its taboos; on ascending the hall, ask about its rites; on approaching a patient, ask what brings them ease.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one bring ease to a patient?' Qibo said: 'When there is heat in the middle causing wasting-thirst disorder, coolness brings ease; in conditions of cold in the middle, warmth brings ease. When there is heat in the stomach, it consumes food quickly, causing the person to feel an emptiness at the heart and to be constantly hungry. When the skin above the navel is hot and there is heat in the intestines, the stool comes out yellow and like gruel. When the skin below the navel is cold and there is cold in the stomach, the abdomen becomes distended; when there is cold in the intestines, there is rumbling in the intestines and diarrhea with undigested food. When there is cold in the stomach and heat in the intestines, there is both distension and diarrhea; when there is heat in the stomach and cold in the intestines, one drinks rapidly and there is pain and distension in the lower abdomen.'

Huangdi said: 'When the stomach desires a cold drink but the intestines desire a hot drink, the two are in mutual opposition — how then does one bring ease? Moreover, princes and nobles and great men, rulers who feast on meat, are arrogant and self-indulgent, following their desires and slighting others, so that no one is able to restrain them; to restrain them would go against their will, yet to comply with them would aggravate their illness. How then does one bring them ease? And in treating them, what should come first?' Qibo said: 'It is the nature of people that none fail to hate death and love life. Tell them of the ruin that awaits them, speak to them of the benefit that is available, guide them toward what brings them ease, and open their understanding to what causes them suffering — even one who is unreasonable, how could such a person fail to listen?'
Huangdi said: 'How, then, does one treat them?' Qibo said: 'In spring and summer, treat the branch first and the root afterward; in autumn and winter, treat the root first and the branch afterward.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one bring ease when the two desires are in mutual opposition?' Qibo said: 'To bring ease in such cases, one's food and drink and clothing should also be suited to cold and warmth — in cold weather one should not be so chilled as to shiver, and in hot weather one should not sweat excessively. As for food and drink, that which is hot should not be scorchingly hot, and that which is cold should not be icy cold. When cold and warmth are moderated and suitable, the qi will thereby be sustained, and evil and deviant influences will not be brought about.'

Huangdi said: 'The chapter on the Root Viscera uses the bodily form, the limbs and joints, and the muscles to observe the size of the five viscera and six bowels. But now, in the case of princes and nobles and great men, rulers who attend court and hold the throne, when they are questioned, who would be permitted to touch and examine them before giving an answer?' Qibo said: 'The bodily form and the limbs and joints are the outer covering of the viscera and bowels — this is not the same as inspection of the face.'

Huangdi said: 'I already know how the qi of the five viscera may be inspected in the face — but how does one come to know and inspect them by means of the limbs and joints?' Qibo said: 'Among the five viscera and six bowels, the lung serves as their canopy; a broad, prominent shoulder and a sunken throat are signs by which its condition can be observed externally.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Qibo said: 'Among the five viscera and six bowels, the heart serves as their ruler; the supraclavicular fossa is its pathway, and a well-developed breastbone allows one to observe the condition of the xiphoid process.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'
Qibo said: 'The liver serves as the general among the viscera, sending its influence to be observed externally; to know its firmness, observe the size of the eyes.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Qibo said: 'The spleen serves as a guard, receiving grain and provisions; observe the condition of the lips and tongue, whether favorable or unfavorable, to know whether the outlook is fortunate or unfortunate.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Qibo said: 'The kidney governs the exterior, extending hearing into the distance; observe the condition of the ears, whether favorable or unfavorable, to know its nature.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I wish to hear of the signs of the six bowels.'

Qibo said: 'Among the six bowels, the stomach serves as their sea; a broad frame, a thick neck, and an expansive chest allow the five grains to be well contained. A long nasal passage indicates the condition of the large intestine. Thick lips and a long philtrum indicate the condition of the small intestine. Full pouches beneath the eyes indicate that the gallbladder is vigorous. Nostrils that flare outward indicate that the bladder leaks and fails to hold urine. A prominent ridge at the center of the nasal bridge indicates that the triple burner is well restrained. These are the signs by which the six bowels are observed. When the upper, middle, and lower thirds of the body are all in proportion, the viscera are at peace and in good condition.'

Huangdi said: 'I have heard that the human being possesses essence, qi, fluid, humor, blood, and vessels. In my own thinking I had supposed these to be but a single qi; yet now they are distinguished into six separate names, and I do not understand the reason for this.' Qibo said: 'When the two spirits contend and unite to form the body, that which is always generated prior to the body itself is called essence. What is called qi?' Qibo said: 'When the upper burner opens and disperses, spreading abroad the flavors of the five grains, warming the skin, filling the body, and moistening the body hair — like the irrigation of mist and dew — this is called qi. What is called fluid?' Qibo said: 'When the interstices release and open, so that sweat pours out profusely, this is called jin — the thin, readily-flowing fluid that vents outward as sweat. What is called humor?' Qibo said: 'When grain enters and the qi becomes full, and a rich moistness pours into the bones, so that the joints can flex and extend, this moistness supplementing and benefiting the brain and marrow so that the skin becomes lustrous, this is called ye — the thicker, denser humor. What is called blood?' Qibo said: 'When the middle burner receives qi and, taking up the fluid, transforms it and turns it red, this is called blood. What is called the vessel?' Qibo said: 'That which contains and restrains the nutritive qi, so that it has nowhere to escape, this is called the vessel.'

Huangdi said: 'Among these six qi there is surplus and insufficiency, abundance and scarcity of qi, deficiency and excess of the brain and marrow, and clarity and turbidity of the blood vessels — how does one come to know these?' Qibo said: 'When essence is lost, there is deafness. When qi is lost, the eyes become dim. When fluid is lost, the interstices open and sweat pours out excessively. When humor is lost, the joints lose their ease of flexion and extension, the complexion becomes withered, the brain and marrow waste away, the lower legs become impeded, and the ears ring repeatedly. When blood is lost, the complexion turns pale, withered and without luster, and the pulse becomes hollow and empty — these are the signs of loss.'

Huangdi said: 'As for these six qi, which is more precious and which is less so?' Qibo said: 'Each of the six qi has its own region and governance, and their relative precedence, whether favorable or unfavorable, may each be taken as a constant standard; yet it is the five grains together with the stomach that serve as their great sea.'

Huangdi asked Bogao: 'I wish to hear, among the six bowels that transmit grain, of the size and length of the intestines and stomach, and how much grain they are able to receive.' Bogao said: 'Allow me to describe this fully — the measurements of depth, distance, and length by which grain passes in and out. From the lips to the teeth is nine fen in length, and the mouth is two cun and a half in breadth. From behind the teeth to the epiglottis is three cun and a half in depth, with a capacity of five ge. The tongue weighs ten liang, is seven cun in length and two cun and a half in breadth. The throat-gate weighs ten liang and is one cun and a half in breadth. From there to the stomach is one chi and six cun in length; the stomach itself is coiled and bent, and when straightened out measures two chi and six cun in length, one chi and five cun in breadth, with a diameter of five cun, and a capacity of three dou and five sheng. The small intestine lies behind, attached to the spine, coiling to the left and folding back upon itself in layered turns; where it empties into the large intestine, it is attached externally above the navel. It winds through sixteen coils in all, two cun and a half in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than eight fen, and a length of three zhang and three chi. The large intestine coils to the left of the navel, winding round and folding downward in layered turns, circling back through sixteen coils, four cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than one cun, and a length of two zhang and one chi. The rectum runs along the spine, receiving what comes from the large intestine, coiling to the left and folding above and below the spine, eight cun across, with a diameter of a little more than two cun, and a length of two chi and eight cun. From the point where the intestines and stomach receive food to the point where it is expelled, the total length is six zhang, four chi and four fen, winding through thirty-two coils in all.'

title_en: On the Ordinary Person Going Without Food
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear why it is that when a person does not eat, death follows within seven days.' Bogao said: 'Allow your servant to explain the reason.'

The stomach is one chi and five cun in breadth, with a diameter of five cun and a length of two chi and six cun; coiled crosswise, it holds three dou and five sheng of water and grain. Of this, the grain that ordinarily remains within amounts to two dou, and the water one dou and five sheng, when full. The upper burner releases qi, sending forth the essential and subtle portion, swift and smooth and rapid in its movement, while the lower burner irrigates the intestines below.

The small intestine is two cun and a half in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than eight fen, and a length of three zhang and two chi; it holds two dou and four sheng of grain, and a little more than six sheng and three ge of water.
The large intestine is four cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little less than one cun, and a length of two zhang and one chi; it holds one dou of grain and seven sheng and a half of water.
The rectum is eight cun in breadth, with a diameter of a little more than two cun, and a length of two chi and eight cun; it holds nine sheng, three ge, and an eighth of a ge of grain.

Altogether, the length of the intestines and stomach is five zhang, eight chi, and four cun, and together they hold nine dou, two sheng, and a little more than one ge of water and grain — this is the total measure of water and grain that the intestines and stomach can receive. In the healthy person, however, matters do not remain fixed in this way: when the stomach is full the intestines are empty, and when the intestines are full the stomach is empty; emptiness and fullness alternate, and so the qi is able to move up and down, the five viscera remain settled and at peace, the blood and vessels are harmonious and free-flowing, and the essence and spirit thereby find their dwelling place. Hence spirit is nothing other than the essential qi of water and grain. Thus, within the intestines and stomach, there ordinarily remains two dou of grain and one dou and five sheng of water. Hence the healthy person has two bowel movements each day, each releasing two and a half sheng, five sheng in the course of a day; over seven days, five times seven gives three dou and five sheng, and the retained water and grain would then be entirely exhausted. This is why, when a healthy person goes without food or drink for seven days, death follows — because the essential qi and fluid and humor derived from grain have all become exhausted.

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard the methods of needling from you, Master, and what you have spoken of has never strayed from nutritive qi, defensive qi, blood, and qi. Now the twelve channel vessels are internally connected to the bowels and viscera, and externally netted throughout the limbs and joints — do you, Master, also correlate these with the four seas?' Qibo replied: 'The human being likewise has four seas, corresponding to the twelve channel-rivers. The channel-rivers all flow into the sea, and the sea has its eastern, western, southern, and northern portions, which are called the four seas.' Huangdi said: 'How does the human being correspond to this?' Qibo said: 'The human being has a Sea of Marrow, a Sea of Blood, a Sea of Qi, and a Sea of Water and Grain — these four correspond to the four seas.'

Huangdi said: 'How far-reaching this is, Master, that you correlate the human being with the four seas of Heaven and Earth! I wish to hear how this correspondence works.' Qibo replied: 'One must first clearly understand the location of yin and yang, exterior and interior, and the ying and shu transport points; only then can the four seas be determined.'

Huangdi said: 'How does one determine them?' Qibo said: 'The stomach is the Sea of Water and Grain; its transport points lie above at Qichong and below at Zusanli. The Penetrating Vessel is the sea of the twelve channels; its transport points lie above at Dazhu and emerge below at the upper and lower borders of Juxu. The chest center is the Sea of Qi; its transport points lie above and below the pillar-bone, and in front, at Renying. The brain is the Sea of Marrow; its transport point lies above at its crown, and below at Fengfu.'

Huangdi said: 'As for these four seas, what benefits them and what harms them? What allows them to flourish and what causes them to fail?' Qibo said: 'To obtain accord brings flourishing; to fall into opposition brings failure. To know how to regulate them is beneficial; not to know how to regulate them is harmful.'

Huangdi said: 'What are the signs of accord and opposition in the four seas?' Qibo said: 'When the Sea of Qi is in surplus, the qi fills the chest, breathing is oppressed, and the face is flushed red; when the Sea of Qi is insufficient, the qi is scant and insufficient even for speech. When the Sea of Blood is in surplus, one constantly feels as though one's body were enlarged, oppressed and vexed without knowing where the illness lies; when the Sea of Blood is insufficient, one likewise constantly feels as though one's body were diminished, constricted, without knowing where the illness lies. When the Sea of Water and Grain is in surplus, the abdomen is full; when the Sea of Water and Grain is insufficient, one is hungry yet cannot take in food. When the Sea of Marrow is in surplus, the body is light and vigorous with abundant strength, exceeding its usual measure; when the Sea of Marrow is insufficient, the brain spins and the ears ring, the lower legs ache and the vision swims, the eyes see nothing, and one becomes languid and inclined to lie down at ease.'

Huangdi said: 'I have now heard of accord and opposition — how does one regulate them?' Qibo said: 'Carefully attend to their transport points, and regulate their deficiency and excess, taking care not to commit any harm; when accord is restored, recovery follows, but when opposition persists, failure is certain.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Huangdi said: 'The twelve channel vessels are distinguished according to the five phases and divided according to the four seasons — what causes their loss and disorder, and what allows them to be well ordered?' Qibo said: 'The five phases have their sequence, and the four seasons have their divisions; when these accord with one another, order results, and when they oppose one another, disorder results.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by according with one another?' Qibo said: 'The twelve channel vessels correspond to the twelve months. The twelve months are divided into the four seasons. The four seasons are spring, autumn, winter, and summer, each with its own distinct qi. When nutritive qi and defensive qi follow each other properly, when yin and yang are already understood, and when clear and turbid do not interfere with one another — when this is so, all is in accord, and order results.'

Huangdi said: 'What is meant by opposition and disorder?' Qibo said: 'When clear qi lodges in yin and turbid qi lodges in yang, when the nutritive qi follows the vessels properly but the defensive qi moves in reverse, then clear and turbid interfere with one another, throwing the chest into disorder — this is called great oppression. Thus when qi is disordered in the heart, there is vexation of the heart, a silent inwardness, and a hanging of the head in quiet stillness; when disordered in the lung, there is labored breathing with the head bowed and raised, panting and gasping,

clutching at the hands with each exhalation; when disordered in the intestines and stomach, this is sudden turmoil disorder; when disordered in the arms and legs, this brings about reversal-cold of the four limbs; when disordered in the head, this brings about reversal-ascent, with heaviness of the head, dizziness, and collapse.

Huangdi said: 'In needling these five disorders, is there a proper method?' Qibo said: 'There is a proper way by which the disorder is brought on, and a proper way by which it is dispelled; to examine and understand this way is called the treasure of the body.' Huangdi said: 'Good. I wish to hear this way.' Qibo said: 'When the qi disorder is in the heart, select the shu-transport points of the Hand Shaoyin Heart channel and of the Heart's ruling minister, the Pericardium channel; when the qi disorder is in the lung, select the ying-spring point of the Hand Taiyin Lung channel and the shu-transport point of the Foot Shaoyin Kidney channel; when the qi disorder is in the intestines and stomach, select points of the Foot Taiyin Spleen and Yangming Stomach channels, and if this does not relieve it, select Zusanli; when the qi disorder is in the head, select Tianzhu and Dazhu, and if this does not resolve it, select the ying-spring and shu-transport points of the Foot Taiyang Bladder channel; when the qi disorder is in the arms and legs, first remove stagnant blood from the vessels, and afterward select the ying-spring and shu-transport points of the Yangming and Shaoyang channels.'

Huangdi said: 'How is supplementation and drainage carried out in this case?' Qibo said: 'To insert the needle slowly and withdraw it slowly is called guiding the qi. Supplementation and drainage that leave no visible trace are called uniting with the essence. For this condition is not one of surplus or insufficiency, but of disordered qi rebelling against itself.' Huangdi said: 'How true this way is, how clear this discourse! I ask that it be inscribed upon jade tablets, and let it be called "Governing Disorder."'

Huangdi said: 'How does distension manifest in the pulse at the inch opening?' Qibo said: 'When the pulse is large, firm, and rough, this indicates distension.' Huangdi said: 'How does one know whether the distension is of the viscera or of the bowels?' Qibo said: 'Yin corresponds to the viscera, and yang corresponds to the bowels.'

Huangdi said: 'When qi causes a person to become distended, does this occur within the blood vessels, or within the viscera and bowels?' Qibo said: 'All three are involved, yet none of these is the true dwelling place of distension.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the dwelling place of distension.' Qibo said: 'Distension always occurs outside the viscera and bowels; it pushes the viscera and bowels aside, encases the chest and rib-sides, and distends the skin — hence it is called distension.'

Huangdi said: 'The viscera and bowels lie within the chest, rib-sides, and abdomen, stored away like precious vessels within a case or chest; each has its own place and dwelling, and though they bear different names they occupy the same region, and within this one region their qi each differ — I wish to hear the reason for this.' Huangdi said: 'I have not yet grasped the meaning of this; let me ask again.' Qibo said: 'The chest and abdomen form the outer wall of the viscera and bowels. The chest center is the palace-city of the pericardium, the heart's ruling minister; the stomach is the great granary; the throat and the small intestine serve as the conduits of transmission; the five orifices of the stomach are the gates of the villages and wards; and Lianquan and Yuying are the pathways of fluid and humor. Thus the five viscera and six bowels each have their own borders and boundaries, and their diseases each take their own distinct forms. When the nutritive qi follows the vessels properly, but the defensive qi moves in reverse, this produces distension of the vessels; when the defensive qi merges with the vessels and moves along the interstices, this produces distension of the skin. Draining at Zusanli: for cases nearby, one draining suffices; for cases more distant, three drainings are needed. Without inquiring whether the condition is deficient or excess, the skilled physician's task lies in swift drainage.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear the manifestations of distension.' Qibo said: 'In heart distension, there is vexation of the heart, shortness of breath, and restless sleep. In lung distension, there is an empty fullness together with wheezing and coughing. In liver distension, there is fullness beneath the rib-sides with pain drawing into the lower abdomen. In spleen distension, there is a tendency toward foul eructation, the four limbs feel vexed and heavy, the body feels so heavy that one cannot bear the weight of one's own clothing, and sleep is restless. In kidney distension, there is fullness of the abdomen drawing into the back, a feeling of oppressive discomfort, and pain in the lower back and hips. Among the distensions of the six bowels: in stomach distension, there is fullness of the abdomen, pain in the epigastrium, a scorched odor perceived at the nose, interference with eating, and difficulty in defecation. In large intestine distension, there is rumbling and gurgling pain in the intestines, and if additionally struck by cold in winter, this brings diarrhea with undigested food. In small intestine distension, there is a cautious, guarded distension of the lower abdomen, with pain drawing into the lower back. In bladder distension, there is fullness of the lower abdomen together with painful urinary blockage. In triple burner distension, the qi fills the skin, giving a sensation of lightness and puffiness without firmness. In gallbladder distension, there is pain and distension beneath the rib-sides, bitterness in the mouth, and a tendency to frequent sighing.'

In all these various forms of distension, the underlying principle is one and the same: one must clearly understand accord and opposition, and not err in the number of needlings. To drain what is deficient and supplement what is already replete drives the spirit from its dwelling, brings about evil and the loss of what is proper, and leaves the true qi unable to settle — this is the failure of the unskilled practitioner, and when it occurs, it is attributed to fate. To supplement what is deficient and drain what is replete allows the spirit to return to its dwelling, and by long sealing up the void, this is called the mark of the skilled physician.

Huangdi said: 'How does distension arise? From what cause does it come about?' Qibo said: 'The defensive qi, in its ordinary course through the body, runs alongside the vessels and travels through the interstitial flesh, its movement having its proper accord and opposition; when yin and yang follow each other properly, harmony with Heaven is attained, the five viscera renew themselves in turn, the four seasons proceed in their proper sequence, and the five grains are thereby transformed. But when reversal qi settles below, the nutritive and defensive qi become lodged and stagnant, cold qi rebels upward, the true qi and the evil qi assail one another, and these two qi contend together — this is what combines to form distension.' Huangdi said: 'Good. But how may one resolve remaining doubt in this matter?' Qibo said: 'By verifying it against the true condition — when the three aspects converge, the diagnosis is confirmed.' Huangdi said: 'Good.'

Huangdi asked Qibo: 'The Discourse on Distension states that, without inquiring whether the condition is deficient or excess, the physician's task lies in swift drainage — for cases nearby, one draining suffices, and for cases more distant, three drainings are needed. But now there are cases in which three drainings fail to relieve the distension — where lies the fault?' Qibo replied: 'This refers to needling that has sunk into the fatty membrane yet has struck the qi-point precisely. If the needle does not strike the qi-point precisely, the qi becomes internally sealed off; if the needle does not sink into the membrane, the qi does not move; and if it passes above and strikes only the flesh, the defensive qi becomes thrown into disorder, and yin and yang chase after one another erratically. In the case of distension, when drainage is called for but is not properly carried out, the qi therefore fails to descend; if three drainings fail to relieve it, one must certainly change the approach — once the qi descends, the treatment should stop, and if it has not yet descended, one begins again. In this way complete success can be assured — how then could there be failure? In treating distension, one must carefully examine its full extent, draining when drainage is called for and supplementing when supplementation is called for, responding as a drum responds to the drumstick — how then could there be a case that fails to yield?'

title_en: Urinary Blockage and the Five-Fold Differentiation of Fluid and Humor
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'When water and grain enter the mouth and are transported to the intestines and stomach, their fluid separates into five kinds. When the weather is cold and one's clothing is thin, it becomes urine and qi; when the weather is hot and one's clothing is heavy, it becomes sweat; when grief and sorrow cause the qi to converge, it becomes tears; when there is heat in the middle and the stomach becomes slack, it becomes saliva. When evil qi rebels internally, the qi becomes blocked and fails to move, and when it fails to move, this becomes water-swelling. I understand that this is so, but I do not know from what it arises — I wish to hear the explanation.'

Qibo said: 'Water and grain both enter through the mouth; their flavors are five in number, and each flows into its own sea. Fluid and humor each travel along their own pathway. Thus the triple burner releases qi, warming the muscles and flesh and filling out the skin — this is jin, the fluid; that which flows but does not circulate freely is ye, the humor.'
When the weather is hot and one's clothing is heavy, the interstices open and so sweat is released; if cold lingers between the interstitial flesh, it gathers into froth and this produces pain.
When the weather is cold, the interstices close, and the qi and dampness fail to circulate; the water then descends and lodges in the bladder, becoming urine and qi.

Among the five viscera and six bowels, the heart serves as their ruler, the ears as their hearing, the eyes as their watch, the lung as their minister, the liver as their general, the spleen as their guard, and the kidney as the governor of the exterior. Thus the fluid and humor of the five viscera and six bowels all seep upward into the eyes. When the heart is stricken with grief and the qi converges, the heart's connecting vessel becomes tense; when the heart's connecting vessel becomes tense, the lung is drawn upward; when the lung is drawn upward, the humor overflows upward. Now the heart's connecting vessel and the lung cannot remain constantly drawn upward — they rise and fall by turns — and so one coughs and tears flow forth.

When there is heat in the middle, the stomach digests food rapidly; when food is digested rapidly, parasites are stirred and move up and down. The intestines and stomach become distended and full, and so the stomach becomes slack; when the stomach becomes slack, the qi rebels upward, and so saliva is expelled.

The fluid and humor of the five grains, blended and harmonized into a rich, oily essence, seeps internally into the hollows of the bones, supplementing and benefiting the brain and marrow, and flows downward into the inner thighs.

When yin and yang lose their harmony, this causes the humor to overflow and pour downward into the genital region; the marrow and humor both diminish and descend, and when this descent is excessive, deficiency results — hence there is pain in the lower back and back, and aching in the lower legs.

When the pathways of yin and yang qi fail to communicate, the four seas become blocked and closed, the triple burner fails to drain properly, and fluid and humor fail to be transformed; water and grain then travel together through the intestines and stomach, separate out at the large intestine, and lodge in the lower burner. If they cannot seep into the bladder, the lower burner becomes distended, and when the water overflows, this becomes water-swelling. This is the accord and opposition in the five-fold differentiation of fluid and humor.

title_en: The Five Inspections and Five Offices
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked Qibo: 'I have heard that in needling there are five offices and five inspections, by which the five qi are observed. The five qi are the messengers of the five viscera, the deputies of the five seasons. I wish to hear from where these five messengers ought to be observed.' Qibo said: 'The five offices are the means by which the five viscera may be inspected.' Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear where they emerge, so that this may serve as a constant standard.' Qibo said: 'The pulse emerges at the qi opening, and the complexion appears at the bright hall, the bridge of the nose; the five colors emerge in turn, each corresponding to its own season, each according to its constant pattern. When the channel qi enters the viscera in disorder, it must certainly be treated and set in order.'

Huangdi said: 'Good. Are the five colors determined by the bright hall alone?' Qibo said: 'When the five offices are already clearly discernible, the glabella and forehead must be broad and expansive, and only then is the bright hall well established. When the bright hall is broad and expansive, when the flanking screens of the cheeks can be seen from without, when the walls are squared and the foundation high, and the drooping structures extend outward properly, then the five colors will be well ordered — level, full, and broad — and such a person may live to a hundred years. When one sees such signs, needling will certainly succeed, for a person of this kind has abundant blood and qi, and firm, dense flesh and muscle, and so can withstand vigorous needling.'

Huangdi said: 'I wish to hear of the five offices.' Qibo said: 'The nose is the office of the lung; the eyes are the office of the liver; the mouth and lips are the office of the spleen; the tongue is the office of the heart; the ears are the office of the kidney.'

Huangdi said: 'What do these offices allow one to observe?' Qibo said: 'They allow one to observe the five viscera. Thus when the lung is diseased, there is labored breathing with flaring of the nostrils; when the liver is diseased, the corners of the eyes turn green-blue; when the spleen is diseased, the lips turn yellow; when the heart is diseased, the tongue curls and shortens, and the cheekbones turn red; when the kidney is diseased, the cheekbones and forehead turn black.'

Huangdi said: 'From where do the five pulses emerge, and where do the five colors appear, and what is it like when even the ordinary complexion is precarious?' Qibo said: 'When the five offices cannot be clearly discerned, when the glabella and forehead are not broad, when the bright hall is small, when the flanking screens cannot be seen, when moreover the walls are low, the foundation beneath the walls absent, and the drooping corners fall away outward — in such a case, even in ordinary health the person is precarious, how much more so when illness is added.'

Huangdi said: 'When the five colors appear at the bright hall, allowing one to observe the qi of the five viscera, do their positions — left and right, high and low — each have their own distinct form?' Qibo said: 'The viscera and bowels, as they are arranged within the body, each occupy their own place in sequence; their positions left and right, above and below, on the face each correspond to their proper measure.'
